message.content: {"items":[{"title":"Demographic data of the population of the German Democratic Republic (GDR) 1949-1989","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/1.12617","justification":"Contains long-run demographic indicators for the GDR, including fertility, marriages, divorces and mortality by causes of death. While the series end in 1989, they are essential for analyzing reproductive health, family life and specific diseases in the former GDR and for constructing 1990s comparisons for the territory of the GDR."},{"title":"German Family Panel (pairfam), Release 9.0 (waves 1 to 10, 2008-2017)","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/pairfam.5678.9.0.0","justification":"Large-scale longitudinal panel on partnership and family dynamics in Germany with detailed data on fertility, reproductive intentions, partnership histories, and marriage. Includes respondents from the eastern German states (former GDR territory), allowing analysis of family life and reproductive behavior in the 1990s cohorts, even though data collection starts later."},{"title":"Children and Childlessness in East and West Germany 1992","url_or_doi":"https://search.gesis.org/research_data/ZA2397","justification":"Survey conducted shortly after reunification that focuses on fertility, reproductive intentions, and family formation in East and West Germany. Includes respondents from the territory of the former GDR and provides direct information on reproductive behavior and family life in the early 1990s."},{"title":"Microcensus 1991 (Scientific Use File)","url_or_doi":"https://search.gesis.org/research_data/ZA2465","justification":"The German Microcensus is an annual 1% sample of the population. The 1991 wave includes detailed information on household composition, marital status, number of children, and health limitations, with variables for region (East/West) enabling analyses for the area of the former GDR in the early 1990s."},{"title":"Microcensus 1995 (Scientific Use File)","url_or_doi":"https://search.gesis.org/research_data/ZA2630","justification":"Provides mid‑1990s information on family structure, marital status, number of children, employment, and some health-related indicators for residents in Germany, with East/West identifiers allowing analyses of reproductive and family life patterns in the territory of the former GDR during the 1990s."},{"title":"Microcensus 1999 (Scientific Use File)","url_or_doi":"https://search.gesis.org/research_data/ZA3339","justification":"Covers the late 1990s with detailed data on household and family composition, marital status, and basic health status. Regional identifiers enable separate analyses for eastern Germany (former GDR), making it suitable to study family life and some aspects of health at the end of the decade."},{"title":"German General Social Survey – ALLBUS 1991","url_or_doi":"https://search.gesis.org/research_data/ZA1860","justification":"ALLBUS 1991 includes demographic and family-related variables (marital status, number of children, partnership status) and region indicators (East/West). While not primarily a health survey, it supports analysis of family life and marriage among residents of the former GDR in the early 1990s."},{"title":"German General Social Survey – ALLBUS 1994","url_or_doi":"https://search.gesis.org/research_data/ZA2450","justification":"Includes socio-demographic information and items on family and partnership, with East/West variables. It allows comparison of family life and marriage between residents of the former GDR and West Germany during the mid‑1990s."},{"title":"German General Social Survey – ALLBUS 1996","url_or_doi":"https://search.gesis.org/research_data/ZA2800","justification":"Provides mid‑ to late‑1990s data on family status, marriage, children, and regional identifiers. While health content is limited, it is useful for studying family and partnership patterns in eastern Germany (former GDR territory) in this period."},{"title":"German General Social Survey – ALLBUS 1998","url_or_doi":"https://search.gesis.org/research_data/ZA3000","justification":"Contains information on marital status, number of children, and partnership, along with East/West region. Enables analysis of family life and marriage in the former GDR area in the late 1990s; can be combined with demographic health context data from other sources for reproductive-health-related research."}]}
